policy_statement: By 2025, SFDPH will ensure Zuckerberg San Francisco General and Laguna Honda Hospitals achieve a 20% reduction in carbon and water footprints via sustainable food purchasing standards aligned with GFPP values (RPC.2-6).
mechanism_description: SFDPH will implement sustainable food purchasing standards so hospital food procurement aligns with GFPP values, reducing the carbon and water footprints associated with the food they buy.
primary_causal_pathway: Institutional procurement choices → shift to lower-emissions food production/sourcing → ↓ supply-chain (Scope 3) GHG emissions → ↓ atmospheric forcing
causal_mechanism_detail:
(1) Change: A public institution (e.g., hospitals) adopts and set sustainable purchasing standards that alter what foods are bought (e.g., less carbon-intensive items, certified sustainable products, lower-waste sourcing).  
(2) Mediating process: Reduced demand for high-emitting agricultural commodities and practices lowers associated emissions from enteric fermentation, manure management, fertilizer use (N₂O), energy in processing/refrigeration/transport, and potentially land-use change; procurement set can also reduce food waste.  
(3) Climate-relevant outcome: Net decrease in lifecycle GHG emissions attributable to the institution’s food purchases (with ancillary reduction in water footprint).
dominant_pathway_test: The single biggest climate-relevant effect is reduced lifecycle GHG emissions from the food supply chain due to shifting procurement toward lower-carbon foods.
mechanism_classification_reasoning:
1) Dominant causal pathway: procurement-driven demand shift → lower-emission food supply chain → reduced GHG emissions.  
2) Why Mitigation over alternatives: although water-footprint reduction and resource efficiency can be important, the largest climate-relevant effect across policies using this mechanism is emissions reduction (especially from agricultural methane/N₂O and associated upstream impacts).  
3) Edge cases applied: if a program focused only on water conservation without materially changing GHG-intensive foods, it could lean Resource Efficiency; however this canonical mechanism explicitly includes carbon-footprint reduction via sustainable purchasing, making mitigation dominant.  
4) Applicability to ALL instances: any policy that implements sustainable food procurement standards to reduce food supply-chain carbon footprint shares the same primary mitigation pathway, regardless of specific standards (GFPP or similar) or setting.  
5) Note on secondary subtypes: co-benefits (water savings, health, local sourcing, resilience) and the exact emissions sources affected are context-specific and should be assigned at the individual policy level based on evidence.
mechanism_edge_case_notes: If “sustainable” is implemented primarily as local/organic without verified GHG reductions, or if standards mainly target water use, the dominant benefit could shift toward Resource Efficiency; verification and footprint accounting mitigate this ambiguity.
key_indicators: “ensure … Hospitals meet a 20% reduction in carbon and water footprints”; “implementing sustainable food purchasing standards”; “food procurement aligns with the core values of the GFPP”; “By 2025”
co_benefits: Reduced water use/impacts (water footprint reduction); potential public health/nutrition improvements from healthier procurement; potential waste reduction
instance_edge_case_notes: Includes an explicit target (“20% reduction”) which resembles target-setting, but the operative mechanism is procurement standards/specifications; classified as procurement-requirement because it changes purchasing rules to achieve the outcome.
